elements.sent-2411-002: Use of large language models such as ChatGPT (\gptfour) for mental health support has grown rapidly, emerging as a promising route to assess and help people with mood disorders, like depression.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, we have a limited understanding of \gptfour's schema of mental disorders, that is, how it internally associates and interprets symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we leveraged contemporary measurement theory to decode how \gptfour interrelates depressive symptoms to inform both clinical utility and theoretical understanding.
elements.sent-2411-005: We found \gptfour's assessment of depression: (a) had high overall convergent validity ($r = 0.71$ with self-report on 955 samples, and $r=0.81$ with experts judgments on 209 samples); (b) had moderately high internal consistency (symptom inter-correlates $r = .23$ to $.78$) that largely aligned with literature and self-report except that \gptfour; (c) underemphasized suicidality's -- and overemphasized psychomotor's -- relationship with other symptoms, and (d) had symptom inference patterns that suggest nuanced hypotheses (e.g. sleep and fatigue are influenced by most other symptoms while feelings of worthlessness/guilt is mostly influenced by depressed mood).
elements.sent-2411-006: Large Language Models (LLMs), such as OpenAI's \gptfour or Google's Gemini are starting to be actively used by thousands of individuals for mental health support, although we have a limited understanding of their outcomes and limitations.
elements.sent-2411-007: As of January 2024, the ChatGPT store~\cite{gptstore-openai-2024 reported tens of thousands of conversations involving GPT therapy bots~\cite{forbes-elliot-2024 , and approximately 1 in 25 chatbot users seek mental health support as one of their primary uses of LLMs~\cite{motyl-etal-2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: A recent survey in the Journal of Medical Economics revealed that over 1 in 10 clinicians in the USA use ChatGPT, and roughly 50\% are interested in continuing its use for data entry, medical scheduling, or research~\cite{shryock-2023-AI .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The potential benefits of LLMs in the mental health domain are numerous.
elements.sent-2411-010: Mental health care often begins with psychodiagnostic assessment, where employing LLMs could play a crucial role for more timely and effective screening and assessment~\cite{schueller2019use .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Present-day screening tools have limited sensitivity, standardization~\cite{bradford2009missed , and cultural adaptability~\cite{nelson2002unequal, snowden2005cultural , plus people report increased willingness to use online mental health technologies available to them~\cite{schueller2019use .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Further, the ability to administer assessments earlier in the care cycle can address the fact that diagnosis often lags behind the onset of symptoms by over a decade~\cite{wang2004delays, wang-etal-2005-failure, ten2013lifetime .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Earlier detection and treatment could mitigate financial barriers that arise from treating more severe conditions~\cite{rowan2013access, cook2017trends, dahlhamer2016barriers .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, similar to treating mental disorders with unevaluated drugs, unevaluated use of LLMs for mental health support comes with many risks~\cite{stade2024large, derga-etal-2024-ChatGPT .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \captionsetup{width=\textwidth} \caption{Overview of Machine Behavior Evaluation to uncover the structure of depression in \gptfour. (1) \gptfour is prompted with step-by-step instructions on performing depression assessment on human-written essays. (2) \gptfour carried out assessments on data from Gu et al., 2023~\cite{gu2023natural}, containing depression essays written by individuals. The model estimated the severity scores for \phq symptoms explicitly mentioned in the essay, followed by scoring the remaining symptoms that were implicit in the essay. (3) \gptfour's estimated scores and spans are used to infer its internal structure of depressive symptoms, which revealed how the web of symptoms was connected, and what language \gptfour reliably used to estimate depression severity. } \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{assets/PilotFigure_v8.jpeg} \label{fig:pilot}
elements.sent-2411-015: This paper presents a framework for evaluating LLMs' ``representation'' of mental health.
elements.sent-2411-016: To do this, we apply principles from measurement theory~\cite{rust2021modern to infer \gptfour's schema of depression (how it appears to organize, interpret, and relate depressive symptoms).}
elements.sent-2411-017: In the process, we quantitatively evaluate two fundamental questions: (a) how \gptfour identifies and estimates depression symptoms from language, and (b) its latent structure between the symptoms used to form an overall assessment.
elements.sent-2411-018: We utilize this framework to study \gptfour's structural representation of depression for several reasons.
elements.sent-2411-019: First, depression is one of the most prevalent mental health conditions, affecting an estimated 10–15\% of the population throughout their lifetime~\cite{lepine2011increasing, lim2018prevalence , and is the leading cause of disability worldwide~\cite{friedrich2017depression .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Second, there exists a vast body of research on depression's symptoms, structure, and their inter-relationships, providing a strong theoretical reference against which to compare~\cite{watson2009differentiating, waszczuk2017hierarchical, fried2016good .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Lastly, depression is a highly heterogeneous disorder comprised of cognitive, affective, behavioral, and somatic symptoms~\cite{fried2015depression ; depression is also highly co-morbid with other conditions, including anxiety~\cite{leckman1983panic , substance use~\cite{swendsen2000comorbidity , somatic disorders~\cite{katon1982depression and other internalizing conditions~\cite{kessler2005prevalence .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Thus, understanding \gptfour's schema of depression suggests generalizations to many aspects and forms of psychopathology.
elements.sent-2411-023: While previous work on how \gptfour's assessments are formed is limited, recent studies have evaluated \gptfour's ability to predict mental disorder~\cite{shin2024using .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Empirical approaches that seek to explain \gptfour's behavior are often limited to performance benchmarks and comparative studies~\cite{markowitz_silicon_2024 , which measure the agreement between model's responses and those of humans~\cite{hagendorff2023human, galatzerlevy2023capability or experts~\cite{matero-etal-2019-suicide .}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Such methods can reveal what the model can do but do not explain how it performs such tasks.
elements.sent-2411-026: The responsible use of LLMs, particularly in critical applications that involve human health and well-being, requires understanding how they perform tasks —-- questions that have traditionally fallen within the purview of fields like psychology and cognitive science.
elements.sent-2411-027: Over the years, psychology has developed and continually refined tools and methods to measure, interpret, and understand the human mind using mathematical and empirical methods designed with the intent of explaining human mental functioning through behavioral observation~\cite{galatzer-levy-etal-2023-machine .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Such methods have uncovered schemas --- the internal concepts underlying a process --- embedded within the neural system from observations of human behaviors~\cite{stein1992schemas, schmidt1995schema, waters2006attachment .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Drawing parallels from cognitive and behavioral psychology, our experiments are designed to collect extensive observations of \gptfour's ability to assess depression.
elements.sent-2411-030: We analyze its generated text (responses) and the input prompts (stimuli) to infer \gptfour's schema of depression.
elements.sent-2411-031: This inferred schema is used to explain \gptfour's behavior in assessing depression~\cite{rahwan2019machine .}
elements.sent-2411-032: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to perform machine psychology~\cite{hagendorff2024machinepsychology to explain \gptfour's functioning.}
elements.sent-2411-033: We employ a robust methodological framework that can be extended to other LLMs to understand how psychopathology is represented within them.
elements.sent-2411-034: This is depicted in \autoref{fig:pilot and described extensively under Methods (\S\ref{sec:methods ).}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The unique contributions of this study center on explaining \gptfour's behavior when identifying depression risk within open-ended essays.
elements.sent-2411-036: In a sample of 955 individuals, we employ measurement theory to infer \gptfour's schema of depression by analyzing (a) the relationships among key depression symptoms and (b) the role of each symptom in determining the degree of depression within the language model.
elements.sent-2411-037: This inferred schema is compared with one derived from self-reported scores to quantitatively identify similarities and differences in the conceptualization of depression as a network of interconnected symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-038: We used multivariate linear regression to assess how \gptfour integrates explicitly mentioned factors in essays to estimate risks for unmentioned factors.
elements.sent-2411-039: We then applied differential language analysis~\cite[DLA]{schwartz2013personality to identify the linguistic cues associated with \gptfour's identification of depressive risk factors.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Lastly, we evaluated whether \gptfour shows evidence that it uses the phrases that it cited as explanations for producing its depression scores.
elements.sent-2411-041: This process involved two key steps.
elements.sent-2411-042: In the first step, the model identified symptoms explicitly mentioned in the participants' essays, citing the corresponding text spans and assigning severity scores to each explicitly mentioned symptom.
elements.sent-2411-043: In the second step, the model inferred any remaining \phq symptoms that were not directly mentioned in the essays but could be implied from the context, providing a free-form rationale for each inferred symptom, along with a severity estimate.
elements.sent-2411-044: To validate these estimates, participants also completed the \phq questionnaire, which assessed their symptom severity over the previous two weeks.
elements.sent-2411-045: Additionally, a random subsample of 209 participant responses was manually annotated by two psychology experts using a similar annotation process to that given to \gptfour.
elements.sent-2411-046: The comparison between GPT-4’s estimates and the participants' self-reported \phq scores was conducted on the full dataset, while the comparison between \gptfour and expert annotations, as shown in \autoref{tab:item_item_corr , was based on the subset of expert-annotated data.}

elements.paragraph-2411-001: Convergent Validity: \gptfour estimates correlate with experts-judgments and self-report.
elements.sent-2411-047: Across all symptoms, the assessors of depression essays (i.e., \gptfour and the experts) demonstrated higher agreement with each other than either did with self-reported \phq scores.
elements.sent-2411-048: The absolute difference between \gptfour's agreements with self-report and experts (difference between first two columns) was less than $0.10$ for all somatic symptoms (fatigue, appetite, and psychomotor agitation/retardation), except for issues related to Sleep ($\Delta=0.15$).
elements.sent-2411-049: All cognitive and emotional symptoms except for anhedonia had a difference in correlation scores over 10 Pearson points.
elements.sent-2411-050: Highest differences were seen with suicidal ideation ($\Delta=0.32$), worthlessness/guilt ($\Delta=0.20$) and concentration difficulties ($\Delta=0.14$).
elements.paragraph-2411-002: Symptom-Symptom Relationships: \gptfour and Self-report have similar inter-symptom covariates overall, except for two symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-051: The symptom-symptom associations from \gptfour estimates as depicted by the heatmap (left) in \autoref{fig:gpt4_sr_item_corr had positive Pearson correlations across the board ($r=0.23-0.78$).}
elements.sent-2411-052: This was in agreement with the symptom-symptom associations from self-reported (right) scores ($r=0.38-0.78$).
elements.sent-2411-053: The effect sizes of the symptom-symptom relationships from \gptfour estimates were similar to self-report for all but two symptoms, namely, psychomotor agitation/retardation and suicidal ideation.
elements.sent-2411-054: In contrast, suicidal ideation was less related to other symptoms for \gptfour than in the self-report.
elements.sent-2411-055: This is evident from the significantly lower association of suicidal ideation with the rest of the symptoms than what was observed in self-reported scores.

elements.paragraph-2411-003: Differential Item Functioning Analysis: \gptfour and self-report have similar discriminant characteristics over latent depression scores.
elements.sent-2411-056: We utilize Item Response Theory (IRT) to conduct a Differential Item Functioning (DIF) analysis on two sets of scores — self-report and \gptfour estimates.
elements.sent-2411-057: The DIF ranks highlight the depression severity ranges where each item most reliably identifies the presence of its symptom.
elements.sent-2411-058: A lower-ranked items are best at distinguishing lower latent scores, and higher-ranked items for higher latent scores.
elements.sent-2411-059: A positive difference in an item's rank between \gptfour and self-report means that language-based assessment of this item by \gptfour is more informative than self-report scores to capture higher depression scores.
elements.sent-2411-060: On the other hand, a negative difference implies that the language-based score of this item has lesser discriminant power than the self-report over higher depression scores.
elements.sent-2411-061: Overall, the distinguishing characteristics of items were in agreement between the two, with suicidal ideation, appetite, and psychomotor agitation/retardation having higher ranks (7-9), and anhedonia, depressed mood, and fatigue having lower ranks (1-3).
elements.sent-2411-062: All items had a very small 95\% confidence interval, showing that these rank estimates have high reliability.
elements.sent-2411-063: Suicidal ideation (rank=9), sleep (rank=4), and anhedonia (rank=3) were the three items with no difference in ranks between \gptfour and self-report.
elements.sent-2411-064: The difference in ranks of other items doesn't indicate that language-based severity scores of \gptfour possessed a significantly different characteristic compared to self-report, i.e. there is a high similarity in the item functioning of self-report and \gptfour for \phq.
elements.paragraph-2411-004: Explicit \& Implicit Symptoms: \gptfour is more precise in estimating explicit symptoms than implicit ones.
elements.sent-2411-065: Convergence of \gptfour with self-report in samples identified with explicit mentions of symptoms was higher than unmentioned samples across all symptoms with an average $\Delta$ of +0.18 Pearson points (\autoref{tab:exp_imp_convergence ).}
elements.sent-2411-066: Particularly, \gptfour's estimation was significantly more ($\Delta\geq0.25$ Pearson points) in agreement with self-report under explicit mention than implicit for anhedonia, depressed mood, and suicidal ideation.
elements.sent-2411-067: Language describing these three symptoms led to more precise \gptfour estimates than language describing other symptoms, despite their varying rates of explicit mentions ($range=5.55\%-96.44\%$).
elements.sent-2411-068: The cardinal symptoms, ie., depressed mood and anhedonia, had notably higher rates of explicit mentions in the essays ($96\%$ and $53\%$ respectively) than other symptoms.

elements.paragraph-2411-005: Interplay between explicitly mentioned symptoms and inferred symptoms
elements.sent-2411-069: We examined how explicit symptoms influence implicit symptoms in \gptfour's assessments by calculating the regression coefficients ($\beta$) from a multivariate linear regression model.
elements.sent-2411-070: This linear model predicted implicit symptom scores based on explicit symptom scores while controlling for all other implicit symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-071: When depressed mood was not explicitly mentioned, it was largely inferred from anhedonia ($\beta=.60$).
elements.sent-2411-072: Conversely, anhedonia was influenced by depressed mood ($\beta=.49$) and also by concentration difficulties ($\beta=.24$).
elements.sent-2411-073: Additionally, the cardinal symptoms were common explicit drivers of nearly all other symptoms with large effect sizes.
elements.sent-2411-074: This suggests that depressed mood and anhedonia are pivotal for accurately estimating the severity of other less specific symptoms of depression~\cite{watson2009differentiating , particularly when they are not directly mentioned in the text.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Furthermore, when sleep disturbances were explicitly mentioned, they had a moderate effect on estimating other somatic symptoms such as fatigue ($\beta=.18$) and abnormal appetite ($\beta=.24$).
elements.sent-2411-076: However, the reverse relationship wasn't significant: sleep disturbances were not inferred from the other somatic markers.
elements.sent-2411-077: Rather, depressed mood emerged as one of its stronger predictors ($\beta=0.26$).
elements.sent-2411-078: Except for fatigue, all other symptoms that were explicitly mentioned more than 10\% of the time —-- including the cardinal symptoms and feelings of worthlessness/guilt —-- had a widespread influence on estimating the severity of other unmentioned symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-079: The explicit mention of nearly all the items predicted fatigue.
elements.sent-2411-080: However, fatigue was a very weak indicator of other unmentioned symptoms, despite being explicitly mentioned in 25\% of the cases.
elements.sent-2411-081: We identified three pairs of symptoms with strong mutual relationships, where the explicit presence of one symptom influenced the implicit estimation of the other, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-082: These pairs include anhedonia and depressed mood, fatigue and psychomotor agitation/retardation, and anhedonia and concentration difficulties.
elements.paragraph-2411-006: Uncovering \gptfour's language of depression: \gptfour generated reliable reasons for diagnosis
elements.sent-2411-083: From the left bar chart in \autoref{fig:words_exp_severity_gpt4 , it is conclusive that \gptfour identified explicit markers (positive Cohen's $d$) of depressive symptoms included words that have been known to be associated with depression from prior studies~\cite{eichstaedt2018facebook, stade2023depression , such as affective words (depressed, feeling low, sad, down), verbs (do), and somatic descriptors (energy, tired, sleep).}}
elements.sent-2411-084: On the other hand, words from non-spans (negative Cohen's $d$) mostly consisted of functional words and a few nouns (e.g. pandemic, home).
elements.sent-2411-085: All these words on the left bar chart of \autoref{fig:words_exp_severity_gpt4 had a statistically significant association ($p<0.05$; tested for multiple hypotheses using Bonferroni correction).}
elements.sent-2411-086: In the right bar chart, 12 out of the 25 words with positive Cohen's $d$ had statistically significant Pearson correlation ($r; p<0.05$) with \gptfour's total estimated \phq.
elements.sent-2411-087: Essays containing `not been depressed' and mentions of `happy' showed a strong negative association with the total score, and phrases like `have been depressed', `sleep', and `low' (as in low energy) had a positive association.
elements.sent-2411-088: From the set of words with negative Cohen's $d$, only `it' had a significant but weak association ($r=0.09$) with the total estimated \phq.
elements.sent-2411-089: This showed that \gptfour doesn't strongly depend on any meaningful linguistic signal outside its cited explicit markers.
elements.sent-2411-090: Receiving professional mental health support is a multi-step, non-linear process that begins with screening and initial assessment.
elements.sent-2411-091: A significant percentage of individuals do not receive timely support due to social barriers and infrastructural limitations within the traditional mental healthcare system~\cite{coombs2021barriers, aguirre2020barriers .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Though effective mental health treatments exist, most individuals with mental disorders do not receive treatment, in part due to shortages of mental healthcare providers~\cite{kazdin2017addressing, konrad2009county .}
elements.sent-2411-093: Digital technologies like \gptfour which possess unprecedented human-comparable skills and may soon be able to practically address many such challenges.
elements.sent-2411-094: With current consumer costs being around \$30/month~\cite{forbes-kell-2024 , models such as \gptfour may be well-positioned to mitigate well-established inhibitors such as high costs, limited mobility, inadequate insurance coverage, and lack of support for parents~\cite{stade2024large .}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Additionally, it can overcome infrastructural limitations, including the need for cultural adaptation and the dearth of mental health clinicians.
elements.sent-2411-096: However, overcoming these challenges does not guarantee precise assessments due to limitations in sensitivity with current measurement tools.
elements.sent-2411-097: To enhance accuracy, these technologies can leverage passive and quasi-passive indicators from daily life~\cite{schwartz2014towards, coppersmith2022digital to form more precise assessments and aid the delivery of timely care to those in need.}
elements.sent-2411-098: While studies have focused on LLMs' generations to identify and measure desirable behaviors for mental health support~\cite{chiu2024bolt, kimmel-2023-chatgpt , the current study instead sought to inform how the internal structure of depression is represented within an LLM by analyzing its behavior with respect to symptoms.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Hence, we tasked \gptfour with identifying the key symptomatic markers of depression expressed within human essays.
elements.sent-2411-100: These essays contained exhibits of causes (e.g. substance use, sedentary lifestyle) and effects (e.g. tired, exhausted) of depression through direct or synonymous mentions (e.g. ``I don't like doing things anymore'' vs ``I don't see the point of getting out of the bed anymore'' both suggest lack of interest).
elements.sent-2411-101: From GPT-4's assessments on language describing depression, we learn how it views the condition to manifest in people, akin to how practicing therapists understand conditions based on the way patients describe it~\cite{frances1995dsm, sommers2012clinical .}
elements.sent-2411-102: We evaluated the viability of \gptfour for assessing depression using two key principles from measurement theory: reliability and validity.
elements.sent-2411-103: We found that \gptfour displays moderate-high levels of concurrent validity and internal consistency (reliability) with depressive severity from a well-established rating scale measure, \phq.
elements.sent-2411-104: The overall result of \gptfour's convergence with self-report was consistent with overall associations of \gptfour~\cite{xu-etal-2024-mental and other \llm s~\cite{galatzerlevy2023capability with standard depression measures, while the item-level results extend convergence to detection of depression symptoms.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Further, we found that \gptfour's generated explanations were generally faithful~\cite{lyu2024towards , with little evidence that it relied on linguistic cues outside its explanations of explicit phrases used to estimate \phq scores.}
elements.sent-2411-106: These findings indicate that \gptfour can be useful at screening patients and identifying symptoms from their language.
elements.sent-2411-107: Language is an information-rich medium compared to rating scales~\cite{kjell2022natural which can provide avenues for making hypotheses about predisposing, precipitating, and perpetuating factors of psychopathology for a given patient while estimating the symptom severity.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Thus, the explanatory output of LLM assessment may free up clinicians to prioritize complex case conceptualization and the creation of carefully tailored treatment plans.
elements.sent-2411-109: Going beyond evaluating \gptfour's convergence with self-reports and experts, we inferred its schema of depressive symptoms from its behavior in assessing depression.
elements.sent-2411-110: This schema, a representation of the psychological constructs, that was derived from \gptfour's responses was depicted as a web of associations between \phq-based depressive symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-111: We found \gptfour and self-report shared commonalities in their schema across the first seven \phq items in relation to other \phq symptoms, while they differed for psychomotor agitation/ retardation and suicidal thoughts.
elements.sent-2411-112: With its effect size being inferred from depressed mood and anhedonia being small ($\beta=.10-.17$; \autoref{fig:exp-imp-gpt4 ), it was not easily inferrable when not mentioned by these prominent explicit items.}
elements.sent-2411-113: However, psychomotor agitation/ retardation had lower convergence with self-report (under both explicit and implicit conditions) and the experts, which showed that \gptfour fundamentally differed in how it inferred this symptom from language.
elements.sent-2411-114: It is also possible that this is a difficult symptom to infer given its weaker relationship to the other symptoms indexed by the \phq (\autoref{fig:heatmap_gpt4_selfreport , \ref{fig:exp-imp-gpt4 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Highly depression-specific symptoms such as anhedonia, depressed mood, and feelings of worthlessness/guilt~\cite{watson2009differentiating, harrison2022psychopathology had both higher base rates of explicit mentions (\autoref{tab:exp_imp_convergence ) and higher effects on implicit symptoms (\autoref{fig:exp-imp-gpt4 ), explaining the severity estimations when symptoms were unmentioned in the essays.}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: Symptoms with lower levels of specificity to depression~\cite{watson2009differentiating , like sleep and appetite, were inferrable in the presence of almost any other symptom.}

elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{Instruction to GPT-4 with an example input text. The instruction given to \gptfour\ for depression assessment comprised three parts: a brief \hlc[SeaGreen!10]{task description}, detailed \hlc[Periwinkle!30]{step-by-step instructions}, and the \hlc[SkyBlue!20]{formatting structure}. \gptfour was prompted with individual depression essays along with this instruction.} \begin{tcolorbox}[ width=\textwidth, colback=white, colframe=black, arc=4mm, boxrule=0.5pt, left=2mm, right=2mm, top=2mm, bottom=2mm, fonttitle= , ] \begin{tcolorbox}[ colback=SeaGreen!8, boxrule=0pt, colframe=white, left=0pt, right=0pt, top=0pt, bottom=0pt, ] Your task is to conduct an in-depth analysis of a provided written text, with the goal of mirroring the psychological state of the author to accurately fill out the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ9). The PHQ9 consists of 9 items that are designed to identify symptoms of depression: \begin{itemize}[noitemsep, leftmargin=15pt, topsep=0pt] \item Anhedonia: Little interest or pleasure in doing things \item Depressed Mood: Feeling down, depressed, or hopeless \item Insomnia or Hypersomnia: Trouble falling or staying asleep, or sleeping too much \item Fatigue: Feeling tired or having little energy \item Poor Appetite or Overeating \item Worthlessness or Guilt: Feeling bad about yourself or that you are a failure or have let yourself or your family down \item Difficulty Concentrating: Trouble concentrating on things \item Psychomotor Agitation or Retardation: Moving or speaking so slowly that other people could have noticed, or being so fidgety or restless that you have been moving around a lot more than usual \item Suicidal Ideation: Thoughts that you would be better off dead, or of hurting yourself \end{itemize} \end{tcolorbox} \begin{tcolorbox}[ colback=Periwinkle!20, boxrule=0pt, colframe=white, left=0pt, right=0pt, top=0pt, bottom=0pt, ] The text should be scrutinized carefully and the following steps should be strictly followed: STEP 1: Pinpoint and record only the PHQ9 items that are clearly stated in the text, assigning each a severity score between 0--3. At this stage, refrain from evaluating or recording items that are not explicitly mentioned. Enclose the text spans indicative of respective symptoms inside single quotes. If multiple spans are present, separate them using comma. STEP 2: For the remaining PHQ9 items not explicitly mentioned in the text, assign an estimated score. This estimation should be based on what you would expect it to be while factoring the severity score of the symptoms already identified. STEP 3: Combine the scores from the previous steps and present the total PHQ9 score as ``Combined Score:''. Categorize this score into only one of the following: None, Mild, Moderate, Moderately Severe, and Severe, and present it as ``Category:''. This will signify the completion of the task. \end{tcolorbox} \begin{tcolorbox}[ colback=SkyBlue!10, boxrule=0pt, colframe=white, left=0pt, right=0pt, top=0pt, bottom=0pt, ] To ensure clarity and easy readability, format your output into a nested JSON. The first level should contain the step number as key (`STEP \#'), and the value should be a JSON containing the items as keys and a list containing reason(s) and the estimated severity score as values. The final step's JSON should contain `Final' as the key and a list containing the summed score and the evaluated category as the value. Here's the abstracted format of the JSON, with elements inside the angle brackets being placeholders for the actual values: \begin{verbatim} { "STEP 1": { "<Item a>": ["<Reason>", "<Severity Score a>"], "<Item b>": ["<Reason>", "<Severity Score b>"], ... }, "STEP 2": { "<Item x>": ["<Reason>", "<Severity Score x>"], "<Item y>": ["<Reason>", "<Severity Score y>"], ... }, "STEP 3": { "Final": ["Combined Score: <score>", "Category: <category>"] } } \end{verbatim} Keep in mind, a score of 0 indicates that the symptom was not experienced, while a score of 3 signifies a high severity level of the specific symptom. \end{tcolorbox} Text: ``I feel overwhelmed because I am bearing the weight of all the domestic and financial responsibilities. I should be getting more help than I am and it makes me feel resentful and annoyed with myself for allowing it to continue. I am avoiding talking to people because my emotions are unpredictable and I worry that the mask might slip.'' \end{tcolorbox} \label{fig:example_instruction_gpt4}
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Example Output of GPT-4. The output from \gptfour\ shows the assessment results in a nested JSON format, following the steps outlined in the instruction. \hlc[Brown!15]{Step 1} contains \phq symptoms identified by \gptfour to be explicitly mentioned in the depression essay, along with the indicative excerpts and a symptom severity score. \hlc[Lavender!50]{Step 2} has all the remaining \phq symptoms accompanied by a free form reason text and an estimated severity score. Finally, \hlc[gray!10]{Step 3} has the combined estimated \phq score and an overall degree of depression category. } \begin{tcolorbox}[ width=\textwidth, colback=white, colframe=black, arc=4mm, boxrule=0.5pt, left=2mm, right=2mm, top=2mm, bottom=2mm, fonttitle= , ] \begin{tcolorbox}[ colback=Brown!15, boxrule=0pt, colframe=white, left=0mm, right=0mm, top=0mm, bottom=0mm, ] STEP 1: \{ \quad Depressed Mood: [`I feel overwhelmed', 2], \quad Worthlessness or Guilt: [`annoyed with myself for allowing it to continue', 2], \quad Difficulty Concentrating: [`my emotions are unpredictable', 0] \} \end{tcolorbox} \begin{tcolorbox}[ colback=Lavender!50, boxrule=0pt, colframe=white, left=0mm, right=0mm, top=0mm, bottom=0mm, ] STEP 2: \{ \quad Anhedonia: [``Based on the overall tone of hopelessness and resentment, it is likely that the author is experiencing diminished interest or pleasure", 1], \quad Insomnia or Hypersomnia: [``No explicit mention, but stress and emotional unpredictability may affect sleep patterns", 1], \quad Fatigue: [``Feeling overwhelmed could imply a state of mental fatigue", 1], \quad Poor appetite or overeating: [``No explicit mention, but stress often affects eating habits", 1], \quad Psychomotor Agitation or Retardation: [```avoiding talking to people' may suggest a withdrawal that could be associated with psychomotor retardation", 1], \quad Suicidal Ideation: [``No explicit mention of suicidal thoughts or self-harm", 0] \} \end{tcolorbox} \begin{tcolorbox}[ colback=gray!10, boxrule=0pt, colframe=white, left=0mm, right=0mm, top=0mm, bottom=0mm, ] STEP 3: \{ \quad Final: [Combined Score: 9, Category: Moderate] \} \end{tcolorbox} \end{tcolorbox} \label{fig:example_output_gpt4}

elements.sent-2411-210: Use of large language models such as ChatGPT (\gptfour/\gptfive) for mental health support has grown rapidly, emerging as a promising route to assess and help people with mood disorders, like depression.
elements.sent-2411-211: However, we have a limited understanding of these language models' schema of mental disorders, that is, how it internally associates and interprets symptoms of such disorders.
elements.sent-2411-212: In this work, we leveraged contemporary measurement theory to decode how \gptfour and \gptfive interrelates depressive symptoms, providing an explanation of how LLMs apply what they learn and informing clinical applications.
elements.sent-2411-213: We found that \gptfour: (a) had strong convergent validity with standard instruments and expert judgments $(r=.70-.81)$, and (b) behaviorally linked depression symptoms with each other (symptom inter-correlates $r=.23-.78$) in accordance with established literature on depression; however, it (c) underemphasized the relationship between suicidality and other symptoms while overemphasizing psychomotor symptoms; and (d) suggested novel hypotheses of symptom mechanisms, for instance, indicating that sleep and fatigue are broadly influenced by other depressive symptoms, while worthlessness/guilt is only tied to depressed mood.
elements.sent-2411-214: These insights provide an empirical foundation for understanding language model's mental health assessments and demonstrate a generalizable approach for explainability in other models and disorders.
elements.sent-2411-215: Our findings can guide key stakeholders to make informed decisions for effectively situating these technologies in the care system.
elements.sent-2411-216: Keywords: Depression, Large Language Models, GPT, Machine Behavior.
elements.sent-2411-217: Large Language Models (LLMs), such as OpenAI's \gptfour or Google's Gemini are starting to be actively used by thousands of individuals~\citep{hua2025scoping for mental health support, although we have a limited understanding of their outcomes and limitations.}
elements.sent-2411-218: A recent survey in the Journal of Medical Economics revealed that over 1 in 10 clinicians in the USA use ChatGPT, and roughly 50\% are interested in continuing its use for data entry, medical scheduling, or research~\citep{shryock-2023-AI .}
elements.sent-2411-219: However, similar to treating mental disorders with drugs that are not understood, there are many risks in using LLMs for mental health support without explanation of how they behave in the domain~\citep{stade2024large, derga-etal-2024-ChatGPT .}
elements.sent-2411-220: This paper presents a framework for evaluating and explaining LLMs' ``representation'' of mental health.
elements.sent-2411-221: To do this, we apply principles from measurement theory~\cite{rust2021modern to infer \gptfour's and \gptfive's schema of depression (how it appears to organize, interpret, and relate depressive symptoms) based on its behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-222: In the process, we quantitatively evaluate two fundamental questions: (a) how does GPT identify and estimate depression symptoms from language, and (b) what is its latent structure between the symptoms used to form an overall assessment.
elements.sent-2411-223: We utilize this framework to study GPTs' structural representation of depression for several reasons.
elements.sent-2411-224: While previous work on how GPTs' assessments are formed is limited, there is growing evidence of these models' ability to predict mental disorder~\cite{shin2024using as compared to self-report questionnaires~\cite{galatzerlevy2023capability or experts~\cite{levkovich2025evaluating .}}}
elements.sent-2411-225: Such methods reveal what the model can do but do not explain how (i.e. explainability) it performs such tasks.
elements.sent-2411-226: The responsible use of LLMs, particularly in critical applications that involve human health and well-being, requires explaining how they perform their tasks.
elements.sent-2411-227: To address the complexities of explaining a massive model, we use a machine behavior approach~\cite{rahwan2019machine .}
elements.sent-2411-228: Over the years, behavioral science has developed and continually refined tools and methods to explain (measure, interpret, and understand) the human mind using mathematical and empirical methods through behavioral observation~\cite{galatzer-levy-etal-2023-machine .}
elements.sent-2411-229: We analyze its generated text (responses) and the input prompts (stimuli) to infer \gptfour's and \gptfive's schema of depression.
elements.sent-2411-230: This inferred schema is used to explain the models' behavior in assessing depression.
elements.sent-2411-231: Rahwan et al., (2019)~\cite{rahwan2019machine and Markowitz \& Boyd (2024)~\citep{markowitz_silicon_2024 call for such an approach to explain GPTs' functioning, and to the best of our knowledge, our study represents the first.}}
elements.sent-2411-232: This is depicted in \autoref{fig:pilot and has been described extensively under Methods.}
elements.sent-2411-233: The unique contributions of this study center on explaining \gptfour's and \gptfive's behavior when identifying depression risk within open-ended essays.
elements.sent-2411-234: In a sample of 955 individuals, we employ measurement theory to infer GPTs' schema of depression by analyzing (a) the relationships among key depression symptoms and (b) the role of each symptom in determining the degree of depression within the language model.
elements.sent-2411-235: We applied differential language analysis~\cite[DLA]{schwartz2013personality to identify the linguistic cues associated with \gptfour's identification of depressive risk factors.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Following this, we evaluated whether \gptfour shows evidence that it uses the phrases it cited as explanations for producing its depression scores.
elements.sent-2411-237: Lastly, we used multivariate linear regression to assess how \gptfour integrates explicitly mentioned factors in essays to estimate risks for unmentioned factors, to uncover its mechanism behind symptom estimation.
elements.sent-2411-238: We further apply this framework to \gptfive and show that performance differences relative to \gptfour on depression assessment are accounted for by shifts in the learned schema, linking aggregate assessment scores to interpretable internal relations among symptoms.
elements.figure-2411-011: \caption{Overview of Machine Behavior Evaluation to uncover the structure of depression in \gptfour. (1) \gptfour is prompted with step-by-step instructions on performing depression assessment on human-written essays. (2) \gptfour carried out assessments on data from Gu et al., 2024~\cite{gu2024natural}, containing depression essays written by individuals. The model estimated the severity scores for \phq symptoms explicitly mentioned in the essay, followed by scoring the remaining symptoms that were implicit in the essay. elements.sent-2411-255: Our analytical approach draws from measurement theory and behavioral analysis to systematically understand \gptfour's and \gptfive's representation of depressive symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-256: We used three strategies to bring out different aspects of how the models processes and integrates depressive symptoms from natural language.
elements.sent-2411-257: We begin by assessing the convergent validity between \gptfour and \gptfive estimates, self-reported \phq scores, and expert judgments using Pearson correlations to establish the basic reliability of language models' assessments before examining its underlying mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-258: Following this, the rest of the analysis in the main paper focuses on \gptfour, and \gptfive's analysis can be found in the Supplementary section.
elements.sent-2411-259: We perform latent structure analysis to model how symptoms are related within \gptfour.
elements.sent-2411-260: Subsequently, we use Differential Language Analysis to gain a deeper understanding of how it views depression in language, and forms severity assessments.
elements.sent-2411-261: Finally, we model the process behind the estimation of implicit symptoms' severity based on explicit symptom estimates.
elements.sent-2411-262: To understand whether \gptfour's assessment behavior reflects symptom associations consistent with established depression research, we computed Pearson correlations between all pairs of the nine \phq symptoms for both \gptfour estimates and self-reported scores.
elements.sent-2411-263: This reveals how \gptfour conceptualizes the relationships among different depressive symptoms compared to how individuals experience and report these relationships.
elements.sent-2411-264: The correlation matrices allow us to identify whether \gptfour has learned symptom associations that align with or deviate from established psychometric patterns in depression research.
elements.sent-2411-265: To contrast the inter-symptom relationships between \gptfour and self-report, we computed the difference between the inter-symptoms relationship matrices.
elements.sent-2411-266: For statistical robustness, this difference matrix was computed over 500 trials by bootstrapped resampling to extract the 95\% confidence interval of the differences.
elements.sent-2411-267: CIs that included 0 were regarded statistically insignificant difference in relationship between the two symptoms $(p>.95)$ across \gptfour and self-report.
elements.sent-2411-269: We conducted Differential Item Functioning (DIF) analysis using Item Response Theory to compare how effectively each symptom discriminates between varying levels of depression severity across \gptfour estimates versus self-reported scores.
elements.sent-2411-270: In IRT, data from ordinal items are modeled using latent variables with logistic link functions~\cite{steinberg2006using .}
elements.sent-2411-271: For this analysis, we used the 1-parameter logistic IRT model (Rasch model)~\cite{rasch1993probabilistic which constrains the slope parameter to 1.0, with item location parameters estimated from responses.}
elements.sent-2411-272: The item location parameter indicates where each symptom functions most effectively along the latent depression continuum.
elements.sent-2411-273: DIF analysis examines whether items function differently across groups by comparing rank orderings of item locations.
elements.sent-2411-274: Lower ranks indicate symptoms that discriminate better at lower depression severity levels, while higher ranks indicate symptoms most informative for distinguishing individuals with severe depression.
elements.sent-2411-275: We computed DIF ranks for both \gptfour and self-report assessments, with rank differences revealing whether \gptfour's language-based assessment emphasizes different symptoms than traditional self-report measures across the depression severity spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-276: To ensure statistical robustness, we estimated 95\% confidence intervals around rank differences using bootstrapped resampling over 500 trials, allowing us to identify statistically significant differences in how symptoms function across assessment methods.
elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Explicit Symptom Identification and Linguistic Analysis
elements.sent-2411-278: Beyond examining \gptfour's numerical estimates, we investigated whether the model's scoring behavior aligns with linguistic patterns known to characterize depression in human language, particularly focusing on language features that extend beyond \gptfour's explicitly identified symptom markers.
elements.sent-2411-279: For each participant's text, we computed 1-, 2-, and 3-gram relative frequencies, categorizing tokens into two groups: those present within \gptfour's explicit spans (text segments the model cited as evidence) and those outside these spans.
elements.sent-2411-280: To ensure robust analysis, we filtered out N-grams used by fewer than 10 participants, and removed any non alphabetic characters (including numbers or special characters).
elements.sent-2411-281: We then computed Cohen's d~\cite{cohen2013statistical effect sizes comparing n-gram relative frequencies within explicit spans versus the remainder of user text.}
elements.sent-2411-282: This analysis identifies linguistic features that \gptfour systematically attributes as an explicit marker of depression.
elements.sent-2411-283: To control for multiple comparisons, we applied Benjamini-Hochberg correction, which maintains acceptable false discovery rates when testing numerous linguistic features simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-284: We further refined the analysis by filtering features using Pointwise Mutual Information~\cite{church-hanks-1990-word with a threshold of 4.0, which identifies n-grams with strong associative relationships to explicit symptom markers while removing spurious associations.}
elements.sent-2411-285: Finally, we correlated the relative frequencies of these linguistically significant features with \gptfour's total \phq scores to determine whether the model's severity estimates rely on language patterns beyond its explicitly cited evidence.
elements.sent-2411-286: Multivariate Regression Modeling of Symptom Inference.
elements.sent-2411-287: To understand \gptfour's mechanism for estimating symptoms that are not explicitly mentioned in user text, we modeled how the \gptfour's implicit symptom scores relate to its explicit symptom identifications.
elements.sent-2411-288: For each symptom $i$ from the complete set of \phq symptoms $P$, we fitted a separate multivariate linear regression model predicting \gptfour's estimated score for symptom $i$, specifically in cases where that symptom was not explicitly identified by the model.
elements.sent-2411-289: The regression models used scores of all other explicitly identified symptoms as predictors, creating a comprehensive picture of how \gptfour integrates explicit information to infer unmentioned symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-290: When a predictor symptom was not explicitly identified, we replaced its score with the dataset mean for that symptom.
elements.sent-2411-291: To ensure statistical robustness and account for sampling variability, we repeated this entire modeling process 500 times using bootstrapped samples of the data.
elements.sent-2411-292: Coefficients whose 90\% confidence intervals included zero were considered statistically non-significant and excluded from interpretation, providing conservative estimates of \gptfour's symptom inference mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-293: First, the model identified explicitly described symptoms, citing relevant text spans from the language sample and assigning severity scores.
elements.sent-2411-294: For the remaining \phq symptoms that were not explicitly mentioned, \gptfour was instructed to provide rationales along with its inferred severity estimates.
elements.sent-2411-295: Participants independently completed the \phq questionnaire reflecting symptom severity over the previous two weeks.
elements.sent-2411-296: Additionally, two psychology experts manually annotated symptoms in a random subsample of 209 responses following the same guidelines given to \gptfour.
elements.sent-2411-297: The symptom-symptom associations from \gptfour estimates in \autoref{fig:gpt4_sr_item_corr (left) had positive Pearson correlations across the board ($r=0.23-0.78$).}
elements.sent-2411-298: In contrast, suicidal ideation was less related to other depressive symptoms for \gptfour than in the self-report.
elements.paragraph-2411-012: Differential Item Functioning Analysis: \gptfour and Self-report have similar discriminant characteristics over latent depression scores.
elements.sent-2411-299: We conducted Differential Item Functioning (DIF) analysis using Item Response Theory (IRT) to compare discriminative power between self-report and \gptfour-derived symptom scores~\cite{stark2006detecting, santelices2012relationship, belzak2020testing .}
elements.sent-2411-300: DIF ranks reflect how effectively each symptom distinguishes between varying levels of latent depression severity: lower ranks indicate better discrimination at lower severity, and higher ranks indicate better discrimination at higher severity.
elements.sent-2411-301: A positive rank difference (\gptfour minus self-report) implies that \gptfour's language-based assessment discriminates better at higher depression severity levels compared to self-report, while a negative difference indicates the opposite.
elements.sent-2411-302: DIF ranks for both methods, along with their 95\% confidence intervals estimated via bootstrapped resampling over 500 trials, are presented in \autoref{tab:irt .}
elements.sent-2411-303: Overall, the discriminative characteristics of items were consistent between \gptfour and self-report assessments.
elements.sent-2411-304: Suicidal ideation, appetite, and psychomotor agitation/retardation had higher ranks (7–9), indicating these symptoms were most informative in distinguishing individuals with higher latent depression severity.
elements.sent-2411-305: On the other hand, anhedonia, depressed mood, and fatigue had lower ranks (1–3), signifying these symptoms effectively discriminated between individuals experiencing milder or lower levels of depression.
elements.sent-2411-306: All item ranks showed narrow 95\% confidence intervals, reflecting high reliability.
elements.sent-2411-307: Suicidal ideation (rank = 9), sleep (rank = 4), and anhedonia (rank = 3) exhibited identical ranks between \gptfour and self-report.
elements.sent-2411-308: Rank differences for other symptoms were minor, reinforcing that language-based severity scores from \gptfour closely align with the discriminative item characteristics observed in self-reported \phq scores.
elements.paragraph-2411-013: Explicit vs. Implicit Symptoms: \gptfour Estimates Explicitly Mentioned Symptoms More Precisely.
elements.sent-2411-309: Agreement between \gptfour estimates and self-report was consistently higher when symptoms were explicitly mentioned, showing an average increase of $+0.18$ Pearson correlation points over implicitly inferred symptoms --— even though explicit mentions were identified in only about one-quarter of instances (\autoref{tab:exp_imp_convergence ).}
elements.sent-2411-310: Notably, explicit mentions significantly improved \gptfour's accuracy ($\geq0.25$ Pearson points) in estimating anhedonia, depressed mood, and suicidal ideation compared to implicit inferences.
elements.sent-2411-311: Language explicitly describing these three symptoms enabled more precise estimates from \gptfour, despite substantial variation in how frequently they were identified to be explicitly mentioned (range: 5.55\%–96.44\%).
elements.sent-2411-312: The cardinal symptoms, depressed mood and anhedonia, had particularly high rates of explicit mentions (96\% and 53\%, respectively), considerably surpassing other symptoms.
elements.paragraph-2411-014: Uncovering \gptfour's language of depression: \gptfour's cited evidences were reliable
elements.sent-2411-313: The left bar chart of \autoref{fig:words_exp_severity_gpt4 shows that words identified by GPT-4 as explicit markers of symptoms (positive Cohen's $d$ values).}
elements.sent-2411-314: These include emotion words (e.g., "depressed," "sad," "down"), verbs (e.g., "do"), and somatic descriptors (e.g., "tired," "sleep," "energy").
elements.sent-2411-315: In contrast, words that GPT-4 rarely cited as explicit markers (negative Cohen's $d$) were mainly neutral functional words and nouns (e.g., "pandemic," "home").
elements.sent-2411-316: All these highlighted words showed statistically significant associations after correcting for multiple comparisons (Benjamini-Hochberg corrected, $p<0.05$).
elements.sent-2411-317: The bar chart on the right further shows that 10 of the 17 explicitly cited words significantly correlated with GPT-4's estimated total \phq scores ($p<0.05$).
elements.sent-2411-318: Phrases such as "have been depressed," "sleep," and "low" (in "low energy") positively correlated with higher severity scores, while phrases like "not been depressed" and "happy" negatively correlated, indicating lower severity.
elements.sent-2411-319: Among words not explicitly cited, only "it" showed a small but significant correlation ($r=0.09$), suggesting GPT-4 primarily bases its severity scores on explicitly cited meaningful linguistic markers.
elements.paragraph-2411-015: Interplay between explicitly mentioned symptoms and inferred symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-320: When depressed mood was not explicitly mentioned, it was largely inferred from explicit mentions of anhedonia ($\beta=.60$).
elements.sent-2411-321: Conversely, anhedonia was influenced by depressed mood ($\beta=.49$) and concentration difficulties ($\beta=.24$).
elements.sent-2411-322: The cardinal symptoms of depression were common explicit drivers of nearly all other symptoms with large effect sizes, underscoring their central role in the network of depressive symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-323: This shows that depressed mood and anhedonia are pivotal for accurately estimating the severity of other less specific symptoms of depression, such as sleep disturbances, appetite changes and fatigue, particularly when they are not directly mentioned in the text.
elements.sent-2411-324: Furthermore, when individuals mentioned sleep disturbances, \gptfour moderately used this to estimate other somatic symptoms, such as fatigue ($\beta=.18$) and abnormal appetite ($\beta=.24$).
elements.sent-2411-325: However, other physical symptoms didn't strongly predict sleep disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-326: Instead, sleep issues were primarily inferred from depressed mood ($\beta=.26$).
elements.sent-2411-327: All symptoms except for fatigue that were explicitly mentioned more than 10\% of the time —-- including the cardinal symptoms and feelings of worthlessness/guilt —-- significantly helped \gptfour to estimate the severity of unmentioned symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-328: However, fatigue was a very weak indicator of other unmentioned symptoms, despite being explicitly mentioned in 1 out of 4 cases.
elements.sent-2411-329: This reflects fatigue’s nonspecific nature, as it occurs broadly across many conditions and isn't uniquely informative for depression severity.
elements.sent-2411-330: Three pairs of symptoms showed strong two-way relationships, each explicitly mentioned symptom significantly influencing the other’s implicit estimation and vice versa: anhedonia and depressed mood, fatigue and psychomotor agitation/retardation, and anhedonia and concentration difficulties.
elements.sent-2411-331: With current consumer costs being around \$20/month, models such as \gptfour may be well-positioned to mitigate well-established inhibitors such as high costs, limited mobility, inadequate insurance coverage, and lack of support for parents~\cite{stade2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-332: While studies have focused on identifying and measuring desirable behaviors of \llm s for mental health support~\cite{chiu2024bolt , the current study instead sought to inform how the internal structure of depression is represented within an LLM by analyzing its behavior with respect to symptoms.}
elements.sent-2411-333: We examined the feasibility of using \gptfour for depression assessment by evaluating two fundamental psychometric properties: reliability and validity.
elements.sent-2411-334: Additionally, \gptfour showed high internal consistency, suggesting it reliably captures the interconnected nature of depressive symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-335: Our findings align with previous research showing that \llm s~\cite{xu-etal-2024-mental, galatzerlevy2023capability generally correlate well with standard depression scales, and we extend these results to symptom-level assessments.}
elements.sent-2411-336: Importantly, \gptfour's cited explicit markers demonstrated both validity and reliability: the linguistic markers it identified aligned with prior findings~\cite{eichstaedt2018facebook, stade2023depression , and were positively associated with its severity estimates, whereas uncited language from depression essays had no meaningful associations.}
elements.sent-2411-337: Such language-based assessments offer richer and more detailed insights than traditional rating scales~\cite{kjell2022natural , which provide avenues for making hypotheses about predisposing, precipitating, and perpetuating factors of psychopathology enabling a more personalized treatment planning.}
elements.sent-2411-338: Going beyond evaluating \gptfour's convergence with self-reports and experts, we inferred its schema of symptoms from its behavior in assessing depression.
elements.sent-2411-339: This schema, a representation of the psychological constructs, that was derived from \gptfour's responses, was depicted as a web of associations between symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-340: We found \gptfour and self-report shared commonalities in their schema across the first seven \phq symptoms, while they differed for psychomotor agitation/ retardation and suicidal thoughts.
elements.sent-2411-341: Specifically, \gptfour’s assessment of suicidal thoughts closely matched expert evaluations and explicit self-reports but struggled to infer it when not directly mentioned.
elements.sent-2411-342: This difficulty highlights the challenge of inferring severe but less frequently mentioned symptoms solely from associated emotional states like depressed mood or anhedonia.
elements.sent-2411-343: In contrast, \gptfour consistently showed lower convergence in assessing psychomotor disturbances, suggesting that these symptoms are less observable through language compared to others.
elements.sent-2411-344: This finding aligns with prior research indicating that objective behavioral signals, such as speech patterns or facial expressions, are more reliable for assessing psychomotor symptoms due to potential recall biases in self-reports~\cite{Sobin1997Psychomotor .}
elements.sent-2411-345: Notably, both suicidal ideation and psychomotor disturbances demonstrated high discriminant power with respect to latent depression severity (\autoref{tab:irt ), underscoring their clinical importance and the need to better understand how \gptfour diverges in modeling these constructs.}
elements.sent-2411-346: Highly depression-specific symptoms such as anhedonia, depressed mood, and feelings of worthlessness/guilt~\cite{watson2009differentiating, harrison2022psychopathology were frequently mentioned in language (\autoref{tab:exp_imp_convergence ) and strongly influenced \gptfour's inference on other symptoms (\autoref{fig:exp-imp-gpt4 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-347: This pattern suggests these core symptoms serve as foundational indicators in \gptfour’s schema of depression, mirroring clinical practice where these cardinal symptoms heavily guide diagnostic reasoning~\cite{fried2016good .}
elements.sent-2411-348: Conversely, nonspecific symptoms, such as sleep disturbances and appetite changes -- common to both depression and anxiety disorders -- could be reliably inferred by \gptfour from the presence of almost any explicitly mentioned symptom.
elements.sent-2411-349: Another important consideration in explaining LLMs is the instructions it was given.
elements.sent-2411-350: As the information in instructions can affect the model's response~\cite{ceballos-arroyo-etal-2024-open , we instructed \gptfour to estimate symptoms of depression before the overall depression severity.}
elements.sent-2411-351: This mirrors studies of LLMs in other domains that have demonstrated improved effectiveness when allowed to break a task into compositional steps~\cite{wei-etal-2022-chain or when instructed to carry out specific modules that culminate into a task~\cite{khot2023decomposed .}}
elements.sent-2411-352: There has been increased advocacy for transparency and explainability~\cite{WHO2021_AI_Health_Ethics to improve public faith and understand the risks it comes with, especially in areas such as mental health support~\cite{lee2021artificial .}}
elements.sent-2411-353: Using measurement theory to arrive at explanatory schemas can inform how LLMs function, where they are bound to fail, and how to effectively situate them in the mental health support to maximize overall benefits.
elements.sent-2411-354: Our evaluation framework is intended to help clinicians and decision-makers gain insights about model capabilities and pitfalls to determine the capacity at which contemporary models can be used in the care pipeline and further improve future systems to yield stronger outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-355: Foremost, the scope of our results is limited to explaining \gptfour's behaviors when depression is conceptualized through the lens of the \phq.
elements.sent-2411-356: While the \phq is a widely used and validated tool, it reflects a categorical, DSM-based view of depression that may not fully capture the complexity of psychological distress.
elements.sent-2411-357: Future work can expand this approach to data-driven, dimensional models such as HiTOP~\cite{kotov2017hierarchical , which may better capture the heterogeneity and complexity of psychopathology across individuals.}
elements.sent-2411-358: Although other modalities beyond language (e.g., speech, behavior) can enrich assessment, we focus on language due to its information-rich nature and the ability of LLMs to leverage this medium for making accurate assessments~\cite{kjell2022natural, varadarajan-etal-2024-alba .}
elements.sent-2411-359: Additionally, our study was limited to a cross-sectional analysis of a relatively small, predominantly UK-based sample.
elements.sent-2411-360: Broader validation across diverse populations and contexts is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-361: Accurate mental health assessments would require taking longitudinal symptom trajectories into account ~\cite{galatzer-levy-etal-2023-machine to overcome the limitations of cross-sectional evaluations, which fails to capture the dynamic nature of symptom progression and individual variability over time.}
elements.sent-2411-362: Although transformer-based models have been effective in encoding longitudinal representations of behavioral~\cite{xu2022globem and health markers~\cite{theodorou2023synthesize , it is vital to understand how effective are LLMs in disentangling both these trajectories over time to assess the mental health.}}
elements.sent-2411-363: This study uncovered the underlying symptom schema within \gptfour that explains how its arrived at scores having high convergence with experts and standard self-report tests.
elements.sent-2411-364: While \gptfour’s schema closely matched self-report for major symptoms, it notably downplayed the link with suicidal ideation and overemphasized psychomotor agitation/retardation.
elements.sent-2411-365: This divergence was somewhat mitigated when suicidal ideation was explicitly mentioned, suggesting it is more conservative when inferring symptoms of suicidality without explicit evidence.
elements.sent-2411-366: On the other hand, its overemphasis on psychomotor symptoms seemed to blanket bias, happening whether it identified evidence or not.
elements.sent-2411-367: With awareness of these discrepancies, the results suggest \gptfour has the potential to assist clinicians, such as in screening to optimize time for those in urgent need.
elements.sent-2411-368: More widely, this work demonstrates how a machine behavior approach yields explanations of large language models' processing of psychological concepts.

elements.figure-2411-017: \caption{Instruction to GPT-4 with an example input text. The instruction given to \gptfour\ for depression assessment comprised three parts: a brief \hlc[SeaGreen!10]{task description}, detailed \hlc[Periwinkle!30]{step-by-step instructions}, and the \hlc[SkyBlue!20]{formatting structure}. \gptfour was prompted with individual depression essays along with this instruction.} \begin{tcolorbox}[ width=1\textwidth, colback=white, colframe=black, arc=2mm, boxrule=0.5pt, left=1mm, right=1mm, top=1mm, bottom=1mm, fonttitle= , ] \begin{tcolorbox}[ colback=SeaGreen!8, boxrule=0pt, colframe=white, left=0pt, right=0pt, top=0pt, bottom=0pt, ] \fontsize{9pt}{4pt}\selectfont Your task is to conduct an in-depth analysis of a provided written text, with the goal of mirroring the psychological state of the author to accurately fill out the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ9). The PHQ9 consists of 9 items that are designed to identify symptoms of depression: \begin{itemize} \item Anhedonia: Little interest or pleasure in doing things \item Depressed Mood: Feeling down, depressed, or hopeless \item Insomnia or Hypersomnia: Trouble falling or staying asleep, or sleeping too much \item Fatigue: Feeling tired or having little energy \item Poor Appetite or Overeating \item Worthlessness or Guilt: Feeling bad about yourself or that you are a failure or have let yourself or your family down \item Difficulty Concentrating: Trouble concentrating on things \item Psychomotor Agitation or Retardation: Moving or speaking so slowly that other people could have noticed, or being so fidgety or restless that you have been moving around a lot more than usual \item Suicidal Ideation: Thoughts that you would be better off dead, or of hurting yourself \end{itemize} \end{tcolorbox} \begin{tcolorbox}[ colback=Periwinkle!20, boxrule=0pt, colframe=white, left=0pt, right=0pt, top=0pt, bottom=0pt, ] \fontsize{9pt}{4pt}\selectfont The text should be scrutinized carefully and the following steps should be strictly followed: STEP 1: Pinpoint and record only the PHQ9 items that are clearly stated in the text, assigning each a severity score between 0--3. At this stage, refrain from evaluating or recording items that are not explicitly mentioned. Enclose the text spans indicative of respective symptoms inside single quotes. If multiple spans are present, separate them using comma. STEP 2: For the remaining PHQ9 items not explicitly mentioned in the text, assign an estimated score. This estimation should be based on what you would expect it to be while factoring the severity score of the symptoms already identified. STEP 3: Combine the scores from the previous steps and present the total PHQ9 score as ``Combined Score:''. Categorize this score into only one of the following: None, Mild, Moderate, Moderately Severe, and Severe, and present it as ``Category:''. This will signify the completion of the task. \end{tcolorbox} \begin{tcolorbox}[ colback=SkyBlue!10, boxrule=0pt, colframe=white, left=0pt, right=0pt, top=0pt, bottom=0pt, ] \fontsize{9pt}{4pt}\selectfont To ensure clarity and easy readability, format your output into a nested JSON. The first level should contain the step number as key (`STEP \#'), and the value should be a JSON containing the items as keys and a list containing reason(s) and the estimated severity score as values. The final step's JSON should contain `Final' as the key and a list containing the summed score and the evaluated category as the value. Here's the abstracted format of the JSON, with elements inside the angle brackets being placeholders for the actual values: \begin{verbatim} { "STEP 1": { "<Item a>": ["<Reason>", "<Severity Score a>"], "<Item b>": ["<Reason>", "<Severity Score b>"], ... }, "STEP 2": { "<Item x>": ["<Reason>", "<Severity Score x>"], "<Item y>": ["<Reason>", "<Severity Score y>"], ... }, "STEP 3": { "Final": ["Combined Score: <score>", "Category: <category>"] } } \end{verbatim} Keep in mind, a score of 0 indicates that the symptom was not experienced, while a score of 3 signifies a high severity level of the specific symptom. \end{tcolorbox} \fontsize{9pt}{4pt}\selectfont Text: ``I feel overwhelmed because I am bearing the weight of all the domestic and financial responsibilities. I should be getting more help than I am and it makes me feel resentful and annoyed with myself for allowing it to continue. I am avoiding talking to people because my emotions are unpredictable and I worry that the mask might slip.'' \end{tcolorbox} \label{fig:example_instruction_gpt4}
